q: How does Nietzsche's will to power differ from Schopenhauer's will to live? Draw on the knowledge base for both thinkers and cite each in the source language.

judge.note: The answer fully states that for Nietzsche life is will to power with self-preservation only indirect; it only partially covers Schopenhauer’s pessimism/denial and Nietzsche’s inversion/affirmation of life/self-overcoming.

misattributions.claim: ‘Воля к жизни → воля к власти’ is an opposition/derivation used to link Schopenhauer’s will to Nietzsche’s will to power
misattributions.note: This frames the relationship as a progression from Schopenhauer’s will-to-life to Nietzsche’s will-to-power, blending them into one directional scheme rather than keeping each thinker’s position distinct.

confusion.note: The answer attributes the comparison slogan ‘вoля к жизни → воля к власти’ as an explicit cross-wiring of doctrines, which blends the thinkers’ positions instead of cleanly contrasting them.

trace.final_text:
According to the knowledge base, Nietzsche’s **“воля к власти”** differs from Schopenhauer’s **“воля к жизни”** in direction and valuation.

- For **Schopenhauer**, the will is a blind metaphysical ground of existence:  
  > “Метафизическое ядро Шопенгауэра: мир есть воля и представление, а вещь-в-себе — слепая, безосновная **воля к жизни**, объективирующаяся во всём сущем.”  
  The same note says this is what generates suffering:  
  > “Именно из неё выводятся страдание”  
  So in the KB, Schopenhauer’s will is blind, grounding the world, and tied to suffering.

- For **Nietzsche**, the will to power is not self-preservation but the active expansion of force:  
  > “Основная гипотеза Ницше о том, что движет живым: не самосохранение, а стремление проявлять силу, расти, присваивать, навязывать форму.”  
  The KB also summarizes Nietzsche’s formula as:  
  > “жизнь есть воля к власти, не самосохранение”  
  So in the KB, Nietzsche’s will to power is an affirmative drive toward growth, appropriation, and giving form.

The comparison note makes the opposition explicit:  
> “**Воля к жизни → воля к власти.** Для Шопенгауэра воля слепа…”  

So, on the KB’s account: Schopenhauer’s will is a blind, suffering-producing “will to live,” while Nietzsche reverses this into a “will to power” that affirms life as growth, strength, and self-overcoming.  

If you want, I can also give this as a compact side-by-side table with only KB citations.

final:
According to the knowledge base, Nietzsche’s **“воля к власти”** differs from Schopenhauer’s **“воля к жизни”** in direction and valuation.

- For **Schopenhauer**, the will is a blind metaphysical ground of existence:  
  > “Метафизическое ядро Шопенгауэра: мир есть воля и представление, а вещь-в-себе — слепая, безосновная **воля к жизни**, объективирующаяся во всём сущем.”  
  The same note says this is what generates suffering:  
  > “Именно из неё выводятся страдание”  
  So in the KB, Schopenhauer’s will is blind, grounding the world, and tied to suffering.

- For **Nietzsche**, the will to power is not self-preservation but the active expansion of force:  
  > “Основная гипотеза Ницше о том, что движет живым: не самосохранение, а стремление проявлять силу, расти, присваивать, навязывать форму.”  
  The KB also summarizes Nietzsche’s formula as:  
  > “жизнь есть воля к власти, не самосохранение”  
  So in the KB, Nietzsche’s will to power is an affirmative drive toward growth, appropriation, and giving form.

The comparison note makes the opposition explicit:  
> “**Воля к жизни → воля к власти.** Для Шопенгауэра воля слепа…”  

So, on the KB’s account: Schopenhauer’s will is a blind, suffering-producing “will to live,” while Nietzsche reverses this into a “will to power” that affirms life as growth, strength, and self-overcoming.
